content.full_text:
ENVIRONMENTAL & UTILITIES
COMMISSION
TELEPHONE
(310) 318-0613

CITY OF REDONDO BEACH
CALIFORNIA
415 DIAMOND STREET
PO BOX 270
REDONDO BEACH, CALIFORNIA 90277-0270

June 12, 1992

John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Wallace:

On behalf of Stephen R. Garfield and Margot A. Nelligan, this letter serves as
a request for an opinion with respect to their duties as members of the
Environmental and Utilities Commission pursuant to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.

Question:
The Commission is currently considering a recycling fee structure for multi-
family buildings with four or more units. Stephen R. Garfield and Margot A.
Nelligan reside in multi-family buildings with four or more units. Is it
appropriate for the two commissioners to participate in the Commission's
consideration of the recycling fee for multi-family buildings?

Facts:
1. The Environmental and Utilities Commission was established in January,
1990 by the Mayor and City Council. The Commission acts as an advisory body
and makes recommendations to the Mayor and City Council on solid waste,
recycling and utility-related subjects. There are seven members on the
Commission. Members serve in a volunteer capacity and are appointed by the
Mayor to serve staggered terms. The Commission meets monthly.

2. Fees for solid waste handling services, including recycling, are
established by resolution of the City Council. Section 5-2.1313 of the Redondo Beach
Municipal Code states, "The City Council will adopt solid waste fees by
Resolution for residences and commercial accounts in such categories as the
City deems appropriate". Resolution No. 7399 established recycling fees at
$1.70 for all residential dwelling units for FY 91-92.

3. Stephen R. Garfield resides in a condominium complex with 12 units.
Margot A. Nelligan resides in a condominium complex with four units.

PRINTED ON RECYCLED PAPER

John W. Wallace
Page 2
June 12, 1992


4. At its May 28, 1992 meeting the Environmental and Utilities Commission
considered a recycling fee structure for multi-family buildings with four or
more units. The staff report is attached.

Representatives from several large multi-family buildings contacted the City
of Redondo Beach stating the current recycling rate structure is inequitable.
The recycling service fee is $1.70/month per unit. These representatives
argue it is easier to collect recyclables since containers are in one
centralized place and a flat rate is unfair.

The contractor is entitled to an established recycling fee of $1.70 per unit.
It was requested that an analysis be conducted linking the recycling fee to
the volume of refuse generated by a building, rather than a flat rate applied
to all units, to determine if this fee structure should be proposed as a more
equitable system.

Analysis reveals that large multi-family buildings will stand to gain with the
implementation of the proposed fee structure. Rates for small multi-family
buildings, primarily eight units and below, will increase more than 100%.

5. The Environmental and Utilities Commission will review the recycling fee
issue and send a recommendation to the Mayor and City Council. The Mayor and
City Council may approve or reject the Commission's recommendation.

Please provide an opinion regarding this matter. If you have any questions,
please feel free to contact Tami Knutson of my staff at (310) 318-0613.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Tami Knutson
Resource Conservation Supervisor

tk

cc: Stephen R. Garfield
    Margot A. Nelligan

City of Redondo Beach
May 28, 1992

1
Recycling Fee Structure

AGENDA ITEM 6A

TO:-        Environmental and Utilities Commission

FROM:      Department of Engineering and Public Works

SUBJECT-   PROPOSED RECYCLING FEE STRUCTURE

RECOMMENDATION:
           The Environmental and Utilities Commission receive and file this
report.

SUMMARY:
           Representatives from two large multi-family buildings have contacted
the City stating the current recycling rate structure is inequitable. The
recycling service fee is $1.70/month per unit. These representatives argue it
is easier to collect recyclables since containers are in one centralized place
and a flat rate is unfair.
           The exclusive solid waste handling services contract provides an
established recycling fee of $1.70 per household, with an expected estimated
revenue to the contractor of $22,275.10/month (13,103 units x 1.70/unit) for
multi-family recycling service.
           Chairman Garfield requested an analysis be conducted linking the
recycling fee to the volume of refuse generated by a building, rather than a
flat rate applied to all units, to determine if this fee structure should be
proposed as a more equitable system. The revenue to the contractor, per the
terms of the agreement, must remain the same in any analysis (number has been
rounded in formula below).
           Linking recycling service to refuse generation yields an increase of
23% for all multi-family buildings for recycling service. The following
formula was used:

           $23,000        Contractual obligation to contractor
           $100,000       Estimated gross revenue for refuse service

           = 23% increase in base refuse rate for recycling service.

           Listed below are a number of examples implementing the proposed
recycling service fee structure. The base rate represents the basic refuse
service cost to the building (i.e., refuse generation). The rate does not
take into account any special services (scout, push-out, etc.) or the "lesser

City of Redondo Beach
May 28, 1992

2
Recycling Fee Structure

of formula" which calculates the building's refuse rate on services provided
before the exclusive contract went into effect.

EXAMPLES OF PROPOSED FORMULA:

Example 1

A large building in Redondo Beach which has 385 units would pay $654.40/month
for recycling service using the current rate of $1.70 per unit per month:

385 units x $1.70/month = $654.00

Linking the building's refuse generation to the proposed recycling service fee
yields a cost of $483.00/month:

$2,100/month (base rate) x 23% = $483.00

Example 2

A building with 54 units would pay $91.80/month for recycling service using
the current rate of $1.70/month:

54 units x $1.70/month = $91.80

Linking the building's refuse generation to the proposed recycling service fee
yields a cost of $71.30/month:

$310.00/month (base rate) x 23% = $71.30

Example 3

A building with 12 units would pay $20.40/month for recycling service using
the current rate of $1.70/month:

12 units x $1.70/month = $20.40

Linking the building's refuse generation to the proposed recycling service fee
yields a cost of $13.80/month:

$60/month (base rate) x 23% = $13.80

Example 4

A building with 9 units would pay $15.30/month for recycling service using the
current rate of $1.70/month:

9 units x 1.70/month = $15.30

Linking the building's refuse generation to the proposed recycling service fee
yields a cost of $13.80/month:

$60/month (base rate) x 23% = $13.80

City of Redondo Beach
May 28, 1992
Recycling Fee Structure 3

Example 5

A building with 8 units would pay $13.60/month for recycling service using the
current rate of $1.70/month:

8 units x 1.70/month = $13.60

Linking the building's refuse generation to the proposed recycling service fee
yields a cost of $13.80/month:

$60/month (base rate) x 23% = $13.80

Example 6

A building with 4 units would pay $6.80/month for recycling service using the
current rate of $1.70/month:

4 units x 1.70/month = $6.80

Linking the building's refuse generation to the proposed recycling service fee
yields a cost of $13.80/month:

$60/month (base rate) x 23% = $13.80

CONCLUSION

Using the proposed recycling service fee structure ties recycling
activity to refuse generation. Multi-family buildings who reduce their refuse
disposal needs can also reduce their recycling service cost since the
recycling fee is directly related to the base refuse rate. The contractual
arrangement requires the contractor be paid $23,000/month or $1.70/month per
unit regardless of the fee mechanism used.

The cost of recycling service under the proposed system would
fluctuate based on a building's refuse disposal needs. The fee will continue
to increase as a building's trash disposal costs decrease from subscription to
less frequent collection or a smaller bin size.

It is important to note there are a substantial number of smaller
buildings (4 to 8 units) in Redondo Beach whose recycling service fee will
substantially increase, in these cases more than 100%.

Number of units          Number of buildings

4 - 8                    1,347
9 and more               282

City of Redondo Beach
May 28, 1992

Recycling Fee Structure

The lowest rate for refuse service is $55.00 (2 cubic yard, once
weekly collection). At some point, a building will not be able to reduce the
cost of their overall waste management bill and a flat recycling service would
need to be established. Small buildings typically pay $60.00 (3 cubic yard,
once weekly collection) as their base refuse rate. Despite having subscribed
to a smaller bin size, their recycling cost will continue to increase since
recycling revenues to the contractor must remain constant.

The contractor has tentatively agreed to implement the proposed
recycling service billing mechanism if appropriate details are worked out.
However, the system would require extensive monitoring as multi-family
building's change their refuse disposal service.

Prepared by:                                     Approved by:

Tami Knutson                                     Desi Alvarez
TAMI KNUTSON                                     DESI ALVAREZ
Resource Conservation Supervisor                City Engineer/Director of Public Works

sections.conclusion:
Using the proposed recycling service fee structure ties recycling activity to refuse generation. Multi-family buildings who reduce their refuse disposal needs can also reduce their recycling service cost since the recycling fee is directly related to the base refuse rate. The contractual arrangement requires the contractor be paid $23,000/month or $1.70/month per unit regardless of the fee mechanism used. The cost of recycling service under the proposed system would fluctuate based on a building's refuse disposal needs. The fee will continue to increase as a building's trash disposal costs decrease from subscription to less frequent collection or a smaller bin size. It is important to note there are a substantial number of smaller buildings (4 to 8 units) in Redondo Beach whose recycling service fee will substantially increase, in these cases more than 100%.

Despite having subscribed to a smaller bin size, their recycling cost will continue to increase since recycling revenues to the contractor must remain constant. The contractor has tentatively agreed to implement the proposed recycling service billing mechanism if appropriate details are worked out.
